coredata.dc:title: Hypochlorite fluorescence sensing by phenylboronic acid-alizarin adduct based carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Simões, Eliana F.C.
da Silva, Luís Pinto
da Silva, Joaquim C.G. Esteves
Leitão, João M.M.
coredata.dc:description: The selective fluorescence sensing of hypochlorite (ClO−) was achieved at pH 7.4 by a simple analytical procedure through the fluorescence quenching of autoclave synthesized carbon dots (CDs), which used as precursor an adduct formed between 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) and alizarin red S (ARS). The use of this adduct allowed the preparation of CDs with a red shifted emission (560 nm) and excitation in the visible range (490 nm). Quantification of hypochlorite was achieved at physiological pH (pH 7.4) in aqueous solutions by fluorescence quenching with a linearity range of 0–200 μM (limit of detection of 4.47 μM, and limit of quantification of 13.41 μM). The selectivity of hypochlorite sensing was confirmed by comparison with other potential analytes, such as glucose, fructose and hydrogen peroxide. Finally, the validity of the proposed assay was further demonstrated by performing recovery assays in different matrices. Thus, this CDs allows the fluorescent sensing of ClO− with spectral properties more suitable for in vitro/in vivo applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
3-Aminophenylboronic acid
Alizarin red S
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: –EEMs and fluorescence emission spectra of the ARS and of the APBA-ARS adduct.
EEMs of the synthetized a) APBA and b) APBA-ARS adduct based CDs.
FTIR spectrum of the APBA CDs and APBA-ARS adduct based CDs.
XRD spectrum of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs.
XPS core level spectra for the APBA-ARS CDs: C 1s (a), O 1s (b), N 1s (c) and S 2p (d).
DLS a) and TEM b) results of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs.
Fluorescence response profile of the APBA-ARS CDs by ClO− addition in the range from a) 0–1000 μM and b) from 0 to 200 μM c).
Fluorescence response profile of the APBA-ARS CDs by ClO− addition in the range from 75 to 200 μM at pH 7, 7.4 and 8.
Fluorescence variation observed by addition of selected related saccharides and ROS/RNS to the synthetized CDs.
ClO− quantification results found in the analysis of sample solutions by the synthetized APBA-ARS based CDs at pH7.4.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
*m – Number of calibration points; R
2 –Coefficient of determination; LOD – Limit of detection; [ClO−] – Hypochlorite concentration; In the estimated concentrations the ± term is the standard deviation of three measurements; RSD – Relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of ClO− quantification by the synthetized APBA-ARS CDs with the quantification by CDs used in previous literature reports.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
* λ
Exc
– Excitation wavelength; λ
Emi
– Emission wavelength; LOD – Limit of detection; QY – Quantum yield.

head.ce:title: Hypochlorite fluorescence sensing by phenylboronic acid-alizarin adduct based carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Research Unit (CIQUP), Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra, Pólo das Ciências da Saúde, 3000-548, Coimbra, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculdade de Farmácia da Universidade de Coimbra

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Research Unit (CIQUP), Faculdade de Farmacia da Universidade de Coimbra, Polo das Ciencias da Saude, 3000-548, Coimbra, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LACOMEPHI, GreenUPorto, Department of Geosciences, Environment and Territorial Planning, Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto, R. Campo Alegre 697, 4169-007, Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: LACOMEPHI, GreenUPorto, Department of Geosciences, Environment and Territorial Planning, Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto, R. Campo Alegre 697, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Research Unit (CIQUP), Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto, R. Campo Alegre 697, 4169-007, Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Research Unit (CIQUP), Faculty of Sciences of University of Porto, R. Campo Alegre 697, 4169-007 Porto, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective fluorescence sensing of hypochlorite (ClO−) was achieved at pH 7.4 by a simple analytical procedure through the fluorescence quenching of autoclave synthesized carbon dots (CDs), which used as precursor an adduct formed between 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) and alizarin red S (ARS). The use of this adduct allowed the preparation of CDs with a red shifted emission (560 nm) and excitation in the visible range (490 nm). Quantification of hypochlorite was achieved at physiological pH (pH 7.4) in aqueous solutions by fluorescence quenching with a linearity range of 0–200 μM (limit of detection of 4.47 μM, and limit of quantification of 13.41 μM). The selectivity of hypochlorite sensing was confirmed by comparison with other potential analytes, such as glucose, fructose and hydrogen peroxide. Finally, the validity of the proposed assay was further demonstrated by performing recovery assays in different matrices. Thus, this CDs allows the fluorescent sensing of ClO− with spectral properties more suitable for in vitro/in vivo applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectrophotometry is extensively used for the direct sensing of different compounds due to its operational simplicity, high sensitivity and fast measurement procedure [1–3]. Recently, fluorescent nanoparticles have gained an increased interest for the fluorescence sensing of different analytes due to their great potential for applications as sensors [4]. More specifically, carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with interesting optical and analytical properties, and so, have gained numerous practical applications in the fields of sensing and bioimaging (among others) [5,6]. Namely, CDs present good chemical stability, tunable fluorescence emission, good water dispersibility, biocompatibility, excellent photostability and low toxicity [7,8]. Furthermore, they can be produced through green and simple methods of synthesis [9], and their ability to be easily functionalized makes them sensitive and selective to specific compounds [10].

Hypochlorite (ClO−) is one of the reactive oxygen (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) with relevance in several physiological and pathological processes [11]. This ROS plays a key role in living systems owing to its strong oxidation and is a powerful microbicide agent in the immune system. Due to its biological value, some effort has been made for the development of fluorescent probes towards its detection. Some ClO− sensors based on CDs have been developed [12–16]. Autoclave synthetized N-doped CDs are sensitive and selective to ClO− through a fluorescence quenching mechanism at an excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 nm of 460 nm, respectively. A response time of 1 min, a linear response range from 0.03 to 15 μM (R 2 = 0.994) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 nM were evaluated [12]. Also microwave synthetized CDs were used for the quantification of ClO− [12–15]. In one of the reported works, microwave-synthetized CDs from sucrose and phosphoric acid were used for the ClO− detection in a linear relationship between (F0/F)/F0 and the concentration of ClO− in the range from 0.2 to 2.0 μM (R2 = 0.994) and a LOD of 15 nM [13]. CDs for the ClO− detection by both down- and up-conversion fluorescence were also synthetized. Two consecutive linear ranges allow a determination of ClO− concentrations with a LOD of 0.05 and 0.06 μM by a down- and an up-conversion fluorescence mechanisms, respectively [14]. Microwave synthesized CDs from citric acid and urea by a pH dependent fluorescence quenching for the ClO− quantification were presented. The response to ClO− is most selective and sensitive at pH 4 and to peroxynitrite (ONOO−) at pH 9. The evaluated LOD was of 0.5 μM for the ClO− [15]. S,N-CDs and P,N-CDs at pH 7.4 are sensitive and selective respectively to ONOO− and ClO−. The ClO− quantification was achieved with the synthesized co-doped P,N-CDs with a LOD of 1.8 μM [16].

The majority of reported CDs based ClO− sensors show emission at lower wavelengths values (blue-green regions under excitation with ultraviolet light). This fact restricts their applications, particularly in the fields of biomedicine because of the blue autofluorescence of biological matrix and of the photodamage of ultraviolet excitation light. The development of red shifted emitting ClO− sensors that could be employed in biological applications, as the CDs presented in this work, have crucial importance. Namely, we will present the development of red shifted emission and ClO− sensing CDs with excitation in the visible range, by using as precursor an adduct formed between a phenylboronic acid (PBA) derivative and a red-emitting dye. Some synthetized phenylboronic acid based CDs with different applications have already been reported [17,18]. For both phenylboronic acid based CDs synthesis the phenylboronic acid was the sole precursor used. In one of the reported works autoclave synthetized CDs were used for the non-enzymatic glucose sensing [18] and in the other synthetized CDs through a modified hydrothermal carbonization shows a herpes simplex virus type 1 antiviral action [17].

PBA derivatives forming reversible cyclic ester bond with diols has been extensively employed in the recognition of saccharides [19–22]. This interaction of boronic acids with saccharides has been investigated towards the development of fluorescent probes with boronic acid motifs [22–32]. The covalent bond formed between PBA and the hydroxyl groups of saccharides (1,2- or 1,3-diols) leads to a five- or six-membered ring structure, which is stable under alkaline conditions [33]. One of the strategies used for a more efficient sensing of saccharides is reacting PBA derivatives with alizarin red S (ARS) [34,35]. The mechanism of the involved reaction has been studied with the water-soluble derivative alizarin red S [36,37]. The connection of boronic acids derivatives with ARS with a formation of an adduct leads to a visible color change of ARS from purple to orange and an increase of the fluorescence intensity of ARS in the red region due to the inhibition of proton-induced fluorescence quenching (Fig. 1 ). This detail allow us the use of the ARS-APBA adduct to synthetize de CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the formed PBA ARS adduct are pH dependent with a maximum fluorescence intensity in a pH range from 6.5 to 8.5. For this adduct two pKa's were evaluated with values of 5 and 11 [36]. ARS has been employed in various studies such as the binding ability of boronic acids with carbohydrates [38,39].

Sensors for ROS and RNS namely the hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [29,40–42] and the ONOO− [43] have been reported based on the oxidative removal of the boronic acid group. A sensor also based on the complexation of PBA with ARS was developed for the detection of H2O2. With this sensor it is observed a red-shifted visible color change, where an on–off fluorescence response indicating the release of ARS and a turn-on electrochemical signal indicating generation of phenol after the reaction with H2O2 [44].

In the present work, an analytical procedure for the detection and quantification of ClO− based on autoclave synthesized fluorescent 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA)-ARS adduct based CDs from was described. The reaction between ARS and APBA with the formation of the adduct allow synthetize CDs with potential to emit at a wavelength more nearest of red, due to the presence of ARS. The sensibility and selectivity detection of the ClO− by the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct based CDs and the ClO− quantification ability were evaluated in reference standard solutions and in real matrices at pH 7.4.

3-Aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA), alizarin red S (ARS), glucose, fructose, sodium hypochlorite, methanol, dimethylformamide, dichloromethane, sodium phosphate dibasic, monosodium phosphate, ammonium acetate, hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchase from Sigma Aldrich. Resistivity water higher than 18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C was used in all the work.

The APBA-ARS adduct based CDs were synthetized by a two-step synthesis. Initially, the APBA-ARS adduct was obtained in organic solution: a solution of APBA 1 × 10−3 M in dichloromethane and a solution of ARS 1 × 10−3 M in dimethylformamide were mixed and kept during 30 min in agitation at room temperature. The APBA-ARS adduct generation was verified by the fluorescence enhancement of the ARS.

After their formation, the APBA-ARS adduct was used as a precursor in the autoclave synthesis of the CDs. For the synthesis, the adduct was transferred to an autoclave and was heated at 125 °C for 180 min. Then the obtained CDs solution was cooled to the room temperature and was posteriorly centrifuged at 12,000 rpm during 30 min to remove suspended impurities. Subsequently, the solvents used were evaporated and the solid obtained was further dissolved in water. The CDs were preserved at 4 °C for further use.

For the ClO− sensing evaluation a phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution was previous prepared with methanol 52.1%, KCl 0.01 mol dm−3 and with KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 0.0028 mol dm−3. The pH of the PBS solution was posterior adjusted with HCl 0.1 M to 7.4 [16,40,45].

To selected volumes of APBA-ARS adduct based CDs solutions and appropriated volume of PBS pH 7.4 different amounts of ClO− were added. The different obtained mixtures were then mixed thoroughly, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 490 nm. The fluorescence intensity was evaluated at an emission wavelength of 555 nm.

The fluorescence Φ was calculated by comparing the integrated luminescence intensities and the absorbance values of the synthesized APBA-ARS adduct based CDs at an excitation wavelength of 520 nm with the ones of quinine sulphate, with the following equation:
Φ =
Φ
R
×
G r a d
G r a
d
R
×
η
2
η
R
2

In the equation, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and η is the refractive index. The subscript R refer to the reference fluorophore, quinine sulphate of known Φ, which is of 0.54 [2].

Total excitation emission matrices (EEMs) and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were obtained in standard 10 mm fluorescence quartz cells and collected in a Jasco FP-8200 spectrofluorimeter in different instrumental conditions. Usually the spectra were obtained with a 1 nm interval and slit widths of 10 nm. Absorbance measurements were made with a JascoV-530 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra were obtained in solid state with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory in a range of 4000 to 650 cm−1 with 16 accumulations and a resolution of 4 cm−1 by a PerkinElmer FITR Spectrum 400. The X-ray powder diffraction spectra were obtained using a powder diffractometer MiniFlex 600 from Rigaku using Cu Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.54,186 Å) and a D/teX-Ultra high speed detector. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Kratos AXIS Ultra HSA, with VISION software for data acquisition and CASAXPS software for data analysis. The analysis was carried out with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.7 eV), operating at 15 kV (90 W), in FAT mode (Fixed Analyzer Transmission), with a pass energy of 40 eV for regions ROI and 80 eV for survey. Data acquisition was performed with a pressure lower than 1 × 10−6 Pa, and it was used a charge neutralization system. The effect of the electric charge was corrected by the reference of the carbon peak (285 eV). The deconvolution of spectra was carried out using CasaXPS programs, in which a peak fitting is performed using Gaussian-Lorentzian peak shape and Shirley type background subtraction.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were made in a Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern. Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) analysis was done in a FESEM (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope) Zeiss – GEMINI II electronic microscopic operating at an accelerating voltage of 30 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct based CDs was done by fluorescence spectrophotometry, FTIR and DLS. An initial fluorescence characterization of the APBA-ARS adduct (prior to autoclave treatment) was also done in order to evaluated the fluorescence conditions for the posterior ClO− fluorescence sensing by the APBA-ARS adduct CDs.

In Fig. 2 are presented the total excitation-emission matrices (EEMs) and the respective fluorescence spectrum at the maximum fluorescence intensity of the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct in organic solvent and of the ARS for comparison.

From the analysis of the EEMs of the ARS and of the APBA-ARS adduct it is possible to see that the EEM of the APBA-ARS adduct present some differences. In what respect the APBA-ARS CDs EEM, beside the common band at an excitation wavelength around 540 nm, could also be found for the APBA-ARS adduct a new band at an excitation of 307 nm and at an emission wavelength of 365 nm. Even so the more relevant is for the APBA-ARS adduct a clear enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of the band related to ARS of 1.57 × , that could allow to confirm the adduct formation.
In Fig. 3 is presented the EEM of the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct based CDs and CDs prepared only with APBA for comparison.

A different EEMs were obtained for the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct and APBA based CDs. In the EEM of the synthetized APBA-ARS CDs, as for the EEM of the APBA-ARS adduct, a new band due to the APBA-ARS adduct appears now at an excitation wavelength of 490 nm and an emission wavelength of 560 nm. Comparing to the EEM of the APBA-ARS alone a decrease of the excitation wavelength from 540 nm to 490 nm and the same emission wavelength of 560 nm it's obtained. The calculated fluorescence Φ of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs found was of 1.8%.
In Fig. 4 are presented the FTIR spectrum of the synthetized CDs with APBA-ARS adduct as precursor and CDs prepared only form APBA as precursor for comparison.

Some differences could be found between the two spectra. The analysis of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs shows a broad band at 3382 cm−1 and a narrow band at 1468 cm−1 characteristic of the –O-H group. Two medium broad bands at 2967 cm−1 and 2766 cm−1 characteristics of the -C-H, a weak broad band at 1640 cm−1 characteristic of the CC bonds and at 1022 cm−1 a band characteristic of the –C-O.

A different FTIR spectrum could be observed for the APBA based CDs. A similar medium broad band at 3432 cm−1 characteristic of –O-H, two weak broad bands at 2931 and 2867 cm−1 related with -C-H bonds and a strong sharp band at 1095 cm−1 related with the –C-O bond could also be observed. Besides that, contrary to the obtained with the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs, a strong narrow band at 1651 cm−1, characteristic of = CO bonds, and a sharp band at 1387 cm−1 related with the –C-N are also obtained.
In Fig. 5 are presented the XRD spectrum of the synthetized APBA-ARS adduct based CDs.

By analysis of the XRD spectrum of the APBA-ARS adduct based CDs it is possible to see some degree of crystallinity with a strong diffraction peaks at 31.5° and 31.6° beside several other lowest peaks that could attributed to the APBA-ARS adduct formation. By magnification of this spectrum in the zone from 5 to 40° it is possible to observe a peak centered to 21° characteristic of the lattice spacing of carbon based materials with amorphous nature [8].

To understand the surface composition and electronic states of the constituent elements present in the APBA-ARS CDs, XPS analysis was carried out as shown in Fig. 6 . The content (in %) of C, N, O and S on the surface of CDs is 74.0%, 5.6%, 18.5% and 1.8%, respectively. Subsequently, it was performed a detailed scan for the C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p internal levels for deconvolution and chemical state.

The C 1s level further splits into four peaks: 285.0, 286.1, 287.3 and 289.0 eV, respectively. These peaks can be attributed to C–C/C–H/adventitious carbon (285.0 eV), C–O/C–N (286.0 eV), CO (287.3 eV) and O–CO groups (289.0 eV), respectively. There is a high contribution from C–C/C–H/adventitious carbon (49.8%) and C–O/C–N (29.4%) groups, while CO groups provide a moderate contribution (16.6%). The contribution of O–CO groups is quite small (4.1%). The deconvolution of O 1s spectrum yielded major peaks at 532.0 eV (57.3%) and 533.3 eV (39.1%), which are due to CO and C–O linkages (respectively). There is also a quite small contribution at 536.1 eV (3.6%). The N 1s spectrum revealed identical contributions from two peaks at 400.8 eV (51.0%) and 402.5 eV (49.0%), which were attributed to amine/amide groups (400.8 eV) and protonated amides (402.5 eV). Finally, the S 2p spectrum revealed two peaks at 168.4 eV (66.7%) and at 169.7 eV (33.3%). The first peak (at 168.4 eV) was attributed to S 2p 2/3 C–S–C groups, while the second group (at 169.7 eV) was attributed to S 2p 1/2 C–S–C structures.
The size of the APBA-ARS CDs was done by DLS and TEM analysis (Fig. 7 a and b) and the shape was evaluated by TEM analysis (Fig. 7b).

By the analysis of the DLS results (Fig. 7a) of the APBA-ARS CDs was found an average diameter of 335 nm and a polydispersity index of 0.423. For the first peak a diameter average of the 490 nm and for the second peak of 130 nm were obtained. The results obtained by TEM analysis (Fig. 7b) showing a spherical or almost spherical shape and average diameter of 57.7 nm (n = 100) with a standard deviation of 8.4. The relatively highest size evaluated by DLS analysis could be attributed to some aggregation of the CDs more evident by DLS analysis.

Attending to the final purpose of the presented work the fluorescence sensing of ClO− by the synthetized CDs was evaluated at pH 7.4 at an excitation and emission wavelength respectively of 490 nm and 555 nm. In Fig. 8 are presented the variation of the fluorescence intensity at pH 7.4 by ClO− addition to the synthetized CDs in a ClO− concentration range from 0 to 1000 μM and from 0 to 200 μM respectively.

An exponential fluorescence intensity decrease was observed with addition of ClO− in the concentration range from 0 to 1000 μM and a linear fluorescence intensity decrease was observed with addition of hypochlorite in the range from 0 to 200 μM. A decrease of the initial fluorescence intensity around 88.5% was found for the highest concentration evaluated (1000 μM). The APBA-ARS CDs shows a good linear response to hypochlorite between a concentration of 0 and 200 μM at pH 7.4.
In order to confirm the chosen pH of 7.4 an evaluation was yet done at pH 7, 7.4 and 8 with the addition of hypochlorite from 75 to 200 μM (Fig. 9 ).

From the results obtained at the three evaluated pH's it is possible to confirm a great quenching effect at pH 7, a slightly lower quenching effect at pH 7.4 and a minor quenching effect at pH 8. A great sensibility was obtained at pH 7.4. Indeed a highest slope of 22 was obtained at pH 7.4 and a lowest slope of 16 and 15 were obtained respectively at pH 8 and 7.

Attending to the previous results regarding ClO− quantification, an evaluation was done in the linear range from 0 to 200 μM at pH 7.4. To ensure the good selectivity for hypochlorite by this method, it was measured the fluorescent response of the CDs in the presence of other compounds. Saccharides, such as glucose and fructose, were evaluated as possible interferents because the PBA and PBA-ARS are employed in the recognition of saccharides. Other ROS/RNS such as H2O2 was also chosen for comparison (Fig. 10 ). Moreover, the different compounds were considered with a concentration of 75 μM.

As expected, ClO− elicited by far the highest degree of quenching. Interestingly, the CDs responded to the other ROS/RNS not by quenching, but by fluorescence enhancement. Contrary to the previous reported with the phenylboronic acid-based CDs, the addiction of glucose and fructose to the APBA-ARS CDs did not lead to fluorescence quenching [10]. That it is probably due to the APBA-ARS adduct formation that inhibits the liaison of the phenylboronic OH groups to the saccharides. In conclusion, glucose, fructose and H2O2 produced only a limited response when compared with ClO−, which indicates the good selectivity of these CDs for ClO−.

The quantification of ClO− in standard solutions and in fortified samples solutions (lemon balm tea and tap water matrices) was made in order to evaluate the quantification capability. The results obtained are presented in Table 1 .

Good quantification results were generally found. Recoveries for the standard and tap water sample solutions near 100% and for the lemon balm tea sample of 74.85% were evaluated. A precision, done by the relative standard deviation (RSD), from 1 to 4% was obtained. Also, a limit of detection (LOD), calculated by dividing 3 times the standard deviation of a blank analysis (n = 10) by the slope of the linear calibration graph, of 4.47 μM was obtained. The limit of quantification (LOQ) found, determined as 3 times the LOD was 13.41 μM.

Also the comparison of the analytical characteristics of the present analytical methodology with previous reported fluorescence analytical methods using CDs was done. In Table 2 is showed the comparison of the fluorescence characteristics and evaluated performance characteristics of the analytical method presented in this work with the analytical methodologies already reported for the fluorescence quantification of ClO−using CDs.

The fluorescence sensing characteristics of the APBA-ARS CDs are generally equivalent to the other reported analytical methods. Even so the presented analytical methodology have a higher limit of detection and a lower fluorescence quantum yield in comparison with the other reported methods. Beside that the presented analytical methodology presents the largest linear range and the possibility of the ClO− quantification in a pH 7.4, physiological pH, at a higher excitation (490 nm) and emission wavelengths (560 nm). These characteristics opening the development of novel ClO− fluorescent sensors with red shifted emission and excitation in the visible region at a physiological pH.

CDs with emission at 560 nm and excitation at 490 nm were obtained by the autoclave treatment of an adduct between 3-aminophenylboronic acid and Alizarin red S. These nanoparticles can be used in ClO− sensing due to a concentration-dependent quenching effect, at physiologic pH (pH 7.4). Good quantification results (LOD of 4.47 μM and LOQ of 13.41 μM) were obtained in standard solutions and in other matrixes (tap water and lemon balm tea). Analysis with potential interferents (such as saccharides and ROS/RNS) supported the potential of these CDs for the selective sensing of ClO−.

Thus, this work opens the way for the development of novel ClO− fluorescent sensors with spectral properties (red shifted emission and excitation in the visible region) more adequate for applications involving biologic tissue.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was made in the framework of the project Sustainable Advanced Materials (NORTE-01-00145-FEDER- 000028), funded by the “Fundo Europeu de Desenvolvimento Regional (FEDER)”, through “Programa Operacional do Norte (NORTE2020)”. Acknowledgment to project POCI- 01-0145-FEDER-006980, funded by FEDER through COMPETE2020, is also made. The Laboratory for Computational Modeling of Environmental Pollutants−Human Interactions (LACOMEPHI) is acknowledged. Luís Pinto da Silva also acknowledges funding from FCT under the Scientific Employment Stimulus (CEECIND/01425/2017). Projects PTDC/QEQ-QFI/0289/2014 and PTDC/QEQ-QAN/5955/2014 are also acknowledged. These projects are co-funded by FCT/MEC (PIDDAC) and by FEDER through “COMPETE− Programa Operacional Fatores de Competitividade (COMPETE-POFC)”.
We thank Centro de Materiais da Universidade do Porto (CEMUP) for performing the XPS measurements (www.cemup.up.pt).
Thanks are also due to Unidade de Controlo de Qualidade de Produtos Farmacêuticos, UCQFarma, Faculty of Pharmacy of the University of Coimbra for XRPD facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current status of modern analytical luminescence methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kulmala, J. Suomi, Current status of modern analytical luminescence methods, Anal. Chim. Acta 500(1) (2003) 21-69.
J.R. Lakowicz, Principles of fluorescence spectroscopy, Springer, New York, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Qu, H. Chen, X. Zheng, J. Cao, X. Liu, Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities, Nanoscale 5(12) (2013) 5514-5518.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based quantum particles: an electroanalytical and biomedical perspective

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging
